docs.title: 🚀 Welcome! You’re in the right place to learn about Databahn platform.
docs.text:
Start exploring features, integrations, and setup guides step by step.

  • Getting Started

    High-level overview of Databahn and its capabilities.

    Start setup

  • SIEM Integrations

    Connect Databahn effortlessly with leading SIEM platforms for unified security visibility.

    View integrations

  • Data Sources

    Ingest security logs from CrowdStrike, Palo Alto, and Entra ID seamlessly.

    Explore connectors

  • Release Notes

    Stay updated with latest features, improvements, and important product changes.

    See updates

docs.title: Product Overview
docs.text:
Databahn is a security data pipeline and security data fabric platform that acts as a control layer between enterprise data sources and downstream security platforms such as SIEMs, data lakes, and analytics tools. It helps organizations collect, normalize, enrich, filter, and route security telemetry before it reaches security operations tools.

The platform is designed to address common SOC challenges such as excessive log volume, noisy alerts, high SIEM ingestion costs, inconsistent log formats, and limited visibility into telemetry pipelines. Instead of forwarding all raw logs directly to SIEM platforms, Databahn processes and optimizes telemetry to ensure that only meaningful and high-value security signals are forwarded for detection and investigation.

Databahn supports telemetry ingestion from multiple security and infrastructure sources, including:

  • Identity platforms such as Microsoft Entra ID
  • Network security devices such as Palo Alto Networks firewalls
  • Endpoint detection and response (EDR) platforms such as CrowdStrike
  • Cloud platforms and cloud-native services
  • Customer applications and custom log sources
  • Security tools, APIs, Syslog sources, and cloud storage pipelines

The platform enables organizations to improve detection quality, reduce operational noise, optimize storage and SIEM costs, and maintain better visibility into log collection and security telemetry pipelines.

docs.title: Data Ingestion and Integration
docs.text:
• Supports ingestion of logs and telemetry from multiple data sources
  • Supports integration with identity, network, endpoint, cloud, and application platforms
  • Supports multiple ingestion methods such as Syslog, APIs, AWS S3/SQS, and cloud-native integrations
  • Enables centralized telemetry collection across hybrid and multi-cloud environments

docs.title: Data Filtering and Noise Reduction
docs.text:
• Removes duplicate and redundant log events
  • Applies rule-based and AI-driven filtering mechanisms
  • Filters low-value or noisy telemetry before forwarding to SIEM platforms
  • Reduces alert fatigue and unnecessary event processing

docs.title: Data Normalization and Enrichment
docs.text:
• Normalizes logs into standardized schemas and formats
  • Enriches telemetry with contextual information such as IP intelligence, user context, geolocation, and threat indicators
  • Improves consistency and searchability of security data across platforms

docs.title: SIEM and Platform Integration
docs.text:
• Supports integration with multiple SIEM and analytics platforms
  • Routes optimized telemetry to downstream security tools and storage platforms
  • Helps reduce SIEM ingestion and retention costs by forwarding only relevant data

docs.title: Pipeline Visibility and Monitoring
docs.text:
• Provides visibility into telemetry ingestion pipelines
  • Detects missing, delayed, or inactive log sources
  • Monitors ingestion health, data flow, and pipeline coverage
  • Helps ensure continuous log availability for security monitoring and compliance

docs.title: Security Operations Optimization
docs.text:
• Improves signal-to-noise ratio for SOC teams
  • Enhances detection engineering and threat hunting workflows
  • Enables more efficient incident detection and investigation
  • Supports scalable security data management across enterprise environments

docs.title: Release notes
docs.text: This section contains the latest release notes for Databahn, including new features, enhancements, and fixes across different versions.

docs.title: Ingest CrowdStrike Logs via AWS SQS / S3
docs.text:
Logs from CrowdStrike are exported into AWS and ingested into Databahn.

Two ingestion options are supported:

Option A: S3 polling (simple) Option B: S3 + SQS (recommended, real-time)

docs.title: Step 1: Enable CrowdStrike export
docs.text:
• Enable FDR or event streaming
  • Configure S3 delivery

docs.title: Step 2: Create S3 bucket
docs.text: Example: crowdstrike-telemetry-bucket

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Databahn S3 connector
docs.text:
In Databahn:

  • Select S3 source
  • Provide bucket name
  • Set polling interval (which can range from 5–15 minutes)

docs.title: Step 4: Validate
docs.text:
• Wait for logs
  • Confirm ingestion in dashboard

docs.title: Step 1: Enable CrowdStrike export
docs.text:
• Enable FDR or streaming export
  • Deliver logs to S3 bucket

docs.title: Step 2: Create S3 bucket
docs.text: Example:crowdstrike-telemetry-bucket

docs.title: Step 3: Create SQS queue
docs.text: Create queue (e.g., crowdstrike-ingestion-queue)

docs.title: Step 4: Configure S3 event notification
docs.text:
• Open S3 bucket
  • Go to Event Notifications
  • Set:
  • Event type: ObjectCreated
  • Destination: SQS queue

docs.title: Step 5: Configure Databahn connector
docs.text:
In Databahn:

  • Choose S3 + SQS ingestion
  • Enter:
  • Bucket name
  • SQS queue URL
  • AWS region
  • IAM credentials or role

Step 6: Validate ingestion

Check Databahn for:

  • Process execution logs
  • Network activity
  • File modifications
  • Detection events

docs.title: Ingest Identity Logs from Microsoft Entra ID using Microsoft Graph API
docs.text:
Use this procedure to send identity and authentication logs from Microsoft Entra ID into Databahn using Microsoft Graph API.

This includes:

  • Sign-in logs
  • Audit logs
  • User activity logs

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Azure/Entra admin access
  • Tenant ID
  • App registration permissions

Required API permissions:

  • AuditLog.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
  • SecurityEvents.Read.All (optional)

docs.title: Step 1: Register application
docs.text:
• Go to Microsoft Entra Admin Center
  • Navigate to App registrations
  • Click New registration
  • Save:
  • Tenant ID
  • Client ID

docs.title: Step 2: Configure API permissions
docs.text:
• Open your app
  • Go to API Permissions
  • Add Microsoft Graph permissions:
  • AuditLog.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
  • Click Grant admin consent

docs.title: Step 3: Create authentication secret
docs.text:
• Go to Certificates & Secrets
  • Create Client Secret
  • Copy and store securely

docs.title: Step 4: Configure Databahn connector
docs.text:
In Databahn:

  • Add new source → Microsoft Entra ID
  • Enter:
  • Tenant ID
  • Client ID
  • Client Secret
  • Set polling interval (recommended: 5–15 minutes)

docs.title: Step 5: Validate ingestion
docs.text:
• Perform a test login
  • Check Databahn dashboard
  • Confirm sign-in logs appear

docs.title: Log Ingestion into Databahn
docs.text:
Databahn ingests logs from three primary categories:

  • Network logs via Palo Alto Networks (Syslog)
  • Identity logs via Microsoft Entra ID (API)
  • Endpoint logs via CrowdStrike (AWS SQS / S3)

Each category has specific access, configuration, and network requirements.

docs.title: Ingest Palo Alto Firewall Data via Syslog into Databahn
docs.text: Use this procedure to ingest logs via Syslog

docs.title: Access Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Admin access to Palo Alto firewall
  • Permission to configure:
  • Syslog server profiles
  • Log forwarding profiles
  • Security policy rules

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Syslog Server Profile
docs.text:
On Palo Alto Networks firewall:

Navigate:

Device → Server Profiles → Syslog → Add

Configure:

  • Name: Databahn-Syslog
  • Server IP: Databahn Syslog Receiver IP
  • Port: 514 (UDP/TCP) or 6514 (TLS recommended)
  • Protocol: UDP / TCP / TLS
  • Facility: LOG_LOCAL0–LOG_LOCAL7

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Log Forwarding Profile
docs.text:
Navigate to Objects → Log Forwarding → Add

Select logs:

  • Traffic logs
  • Threat logs
  • URL filtering logs
  • System logs
  • VPN logs

Attach Syslog profile created above.

docs.title: Step 3: Apply to Security Policy
docs.text:
Navigate Policies → Security → (select rule)

Under Actions tab, select Log Forwarding Profile

docs.title: Step 4: Commit Configuration
docs.text: Click Commit and then click Apply Changes

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Use this procedure to send strong signals to IBM QRadar using Syslog.

docs.title: Step 1: Configure QRadar log source
docs.text:
• Open QRadar Admin
  • Add Log Source
  • Select Syslog

docs.title: Step 2: Get QRadar details
docs.text:
• IP address of QRadar
  • Port (514 or 6514)

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Databahn
docs.text:
• Go to SIEM Integrations
  • Select QRadar
  • Enter:
  • IP address
  • Port
  • Protocol (UDP/TCP/TLS)

docs.title: Step 4: Apply filtering rules
docs.text:
Use this procedure:

  • Only HIGH / CRITICAL events
  • Only confirmed detections
  • Only correlated incidents

docs.title: Step 5: Enable export
docs.text: Activate QRadar forwarding

docs.title: Step 6: Validate
docs.text:
• Check QRadar “Offenses”
  • Confirm Databahn alerts appear

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Use this procedure to send Databahn strong security signals into Microsoft Sentinel.

docs.title: Step 1: Collect Sentinel details
docs.text:
From Microsoft:

  • Workspace ID
  • Primary Key OR DCR endpoint

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Databahn connector
docs.text:
• Go to Databahn → SIEM Integrations
  • Select Microsoft Sentinel
  • Enter credentials

docs.title: Step 3: Map event fields
docs.text:
Use this procedure:

Map Databahn output to Sentinel schema:

  • user → Account
  • ip → IPAddress
  • event type → Activity
  • severity → Level

docs.title: Step 4: Enable export
docs.text: Turn ON Sentinel forwarding

docs.title: Step 5: Validate
docs.text:
• Trigger a high-severity event
  • Confirm ingestion in Sentinel logs

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In this setup, Databahn is the central security data platform.

Databahn:

  • Collects logs from multiple security tools (firewalls, identity, endpoint, cloud)
  • Normalizes and enriches all incoming logs
  • Detects threats and correlates events across sources
  • Identifies high-confidence security signals
  • Forwards only those strong signals to external SIEM platforms

docs.title: Supported SIEM Destinations
docs.text:
Databahn can send filtered security events to:

  • Microsoft Microsoft Sentinel
  • Splunk
  • IBM QRadar

docs.title: Databahn prerequisites
docs.text:
Before configuring SIEM export:

  • Databahn must be receiving logs from all sources
  • Detection engine must be enabled
  • SIEM export module must be active
  • Admin access to SIEM integration settings

docs.title: Microsoft Sentinel
docs.text:
• Log Analytics Workspace created
  • Workspace ID available
  • Primary Key OR DCR/DCE endpoint available

docs.title: Splunk
docs.text:
• HTTP Event Collector (HEC) enabled
  • HEC token generated
  • Index created (e.g., security)

docs.title: IBM QRadar
docs.text:
• Syslog receiver enabled
  • QRadar IP address available
  • Port configured (usually 514 or 6514)

docs.title: Network requirements
docs.text:
Databahn must be able to reach SIEM endpoints:

  • HTTPS (Sentinel, Splunk)
  • Syslog TCP/UDP (QRadar)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Use this procedure to send strong signals to Splunk via HTTP Event Collector (HEC).

docs.title: Step 1: Enable HEC in Splunk
docs.text:
• Go to Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector
  • Enable HEC
  • Create token

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Databahn
docs.text:
• Go to Databahn → SIEM Integrations
  • Select Splunk
  • Enter:
  • HEC URL
  • Token
  • Index name

docs.title: Step 3: Apply filtering
docs.text:
Use this procedure:

  • Forward only:
  • HIGH severity
  • CRITICAL severity
  • Correlated events only

docs.title: Step 4: Enable export
docs.text: Turn ON Splunk forwarding

docs.title: Step 5: Validate
docs.text: Search Splunk index Confirm events arriving
